scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle writes from 4th-c. Athens; his proposals for the best state are rooted in and address the norms of his contemporary Greek civic life.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348), reflecting contemporary views on the lifecycle of citizens in a Greek polis.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's theory of slavery and land tenure is a philosophical systematization of contemporary Greek socio-economic structures.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348); the reliance on alien slave labor was a contemporary reality in his world.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly refers to 'some people' (other Greek city-states) having this law, making it a contemporary external custom.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as an existing law in contemporary states at the time of his writing (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The description of urban planning and reservoir construction reflects contemporary 4th-century BCE Greek engineering and civic policy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a standard or ideal mode of urban defense.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The concern for city walls and the survival of the citizen body reflects the contemporary military realities of 4th-century BCE Greece.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) regarding the necessity of contemporary fortification methods.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly identifies this specific type of 'free agora' as being customary in Thessaly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle cites this as a contemporary custom practiced in Thessaly during his lifetime (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's recommendations for gymnasia and the separation of ages reflect contemporary Greek social organization and educational habits.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a 'noble practice' contemporary to his era.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rotation of political office based on age is a fundamental principle Aristotle discusses within the context of the Greek polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a natural and appropriate social law for his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The definition of a citizen as one who shares in government is the standard 4th-century BCE Athenian view articulated by Aristotle.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a contemporary definition of the citizen in a Greek state.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The educational theory regarding habit and reason represents Aristotle's contemporary pedagogical philosophy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's focus on the 'lawgiver' and marital ages is a response to the demographic and social concerns of his contemporary pollis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a legislative priority for his era.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of slaves from leisure is presented as a fundamental and contemporary social fact of Greek slave-holding society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a common social maxim of his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The curriculum training citizens to rule and be ruled is modeled on contemporary Greek civic participation.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a core educational requirement for his contemporary society.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle refers to infant exposure as a matter of 'regular customs' and proposed laws within the Greek world.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The discharging of seniors from reproductive duty for the state reflects Aristotle's contemporary views on aging and civic obligation.
